speaker: interviewer
text: Rene Firestone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me you got married again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I fell in love with a lovely English girl. She changed [PAUSES] to Judaism, got married. I had my business. [PAUSES] We had a very nice, good relationship. We have no-- we had no children. But unfortunately, about four or five years after I was married, Lynda died of a congenital heart disease with which she was born and nobody knew. [PAUSES] At that time, I really don't-- didn't know what to do anymore. I sold my business. [PAUSES] I sold the house that I had-- that I built in England. I went into an apartment-- on my own, of course-- [PAUSES] in the same town as I'd had my business. I started seeking the sun more. [PAUSES] I started going to Spain. [PAUSES] I spent time in Spain. And really I was at loose ends what to do. I was afraid to get married again, [PAUSES] although I needed the company of women. I like the company of women. I've always been in the company of women. [PAUSES] I had no real man friends, really, men friends. And [PAUSES] I decided I didn't want to stay in England. I'd go on a visit to America. I came here, to Palm Springs, to a cousin of my mother's, who put me up and then showed me a little bit, you know, what was up. And about, oh, I think about a month, or maybe less than that time that I was here-- I came in November 1979. That's right. It was less than a month, because I had a pre-Christmas party to which I was invited with my aunt. I called her 'aunt'. She was second cousin, my mother's generation. [PAUSES] I was invited to this party given by an Austrian man. And there was a young woman there across the room from me who attracted me, who I felt attracted to, and [PAUSES] I started talking to her. And this young woman, Terry Jane is her name, is today my wife. We've been married-- we've known each other since that time, which is now 17 years, and we've been married 11. [PAUSES] She's [PAUSES] an only child, born in Washington state, raised in Long Beach. [PAUSES] Has both her mother and father, although separate, divorced. She has no children, nor does she have brothers and sisters. And [PAUSES] she, I always say, has been the best thing that has ever happened to me, certainly in America. I had to come all these miles, all through my life, to meet her, because she's really you know, an angel. She's an angel, and has what we call a [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother join you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, thank God, is still alive. She's-- was 90 years old in November last. She lives with me and with my wife, Terry. We, the three of us, have lived together since my stepfather died in 1982. It is coming on 14 years that my mother lives with us. [PAUSES] And it is in-- thanks to my wife that that is possible, because my wife really is wonderful to have my mother living with us, as indeed is my need. I need to have my mother with me. It is my last connection to my life, to my past. [PAUSES] And until, until it's no more possible, I will have my mother with me. It's not always easy-- nor for me, nor for my mother, and certainly not for my wife. But I cannot help myself. I have to do this. People have said to me, relatives in England have said to me, put your mother in a nice place. And I can't do it. I need her and she needs me. I don't need know who needs who more-- most, but I have to see it through for my sake, for my late sister's sake, and certainly for my father's sake. And having Terry, that will do that, to me, says it all, absolutely says it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have contact or a relationship with your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, my daughter, who's very well-educated and married, [PAUSES] is a professor in London. She really doesn't want to have a relationship with me at this stage in her life. When I saw her last April for a moment in London when she wanted to see me, she said to me that I'm just a biological connection to her, and in actual fact I'm like a stranger. And she asked me to respect her wishes, that she wants to lead her life, and, using her words, said, 'and it doesn't include you.' Of course, it's a catastrophe for me. But it's much more, I think, a catastrophe for my mother. [PAUSES] You know, my mother cries about it, but there's nothing we can do. [PAUSES] Nothing at all. I've tried so many times over so many years. [PAUSES] At this stage in her life, obviously that is what she wishes. And she's adult enough in years, well-educated. But whether she's educated about the ways of life, that I have my doubts about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last year was 50 years after liberation of the camp that you were in. Have you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you, Rene, what happened to me. I came back from Europe, and I had a phone call from a lady, a survivor, lives in Carmel, called Rebecca [? Segal. ?] She phoned me up and she said-- she's also from Holland, but. She phoned me up. She said there was something on the television. I've videotaped it for you. I'm sending it to you. And it was the Jane Pauley I think it was. I put the thing, the tape, in the machine, and I was absolutely riveted to my chair. There before me I saw on this tape Luba for the first time in 50 years, [PAUSES] Luba living in Miami. The only thing I'd ever known, because of an article in the paper in Holland after the war that Luba had gone to America, and that's all I ever knew. And there I was confronted with this video of Luba in Florida with other children of my group giving her a birthday party. You can imagine how I felt. I was a basket case, a basket case. I'm still a basket case when I think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't long after that, watching that video, that I had a phone call one morning. I pick up the phone, and a man with an accent says to me, are you-- in Dutch he says to me, are you Maurice Rabbie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, it depends who's asking the question. [PAUSES] He introduces himself and says to me, my name is Isaac Rodriguez Cohen. He says, we haven't seen each other for 50 years. I must have been on the phone an hour with this man. He was one of the men in the video. They have gotten my phone number through another child Diamond transport survivor in London, had phoned through the phone book, looked at my original name, found somebody-- a cousin of my mother's with the name Rabbie-- and got my phone number. [PAUSES] He told me that they had discovered in Israel on a kibbutz the original German transportation list with the names of the children. He would fax me a copy, which he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: He then told me that he had contacted 26 surviving children of the Diamond transport group in various parts of the world-- mostly still in Holland, some in Australia, some in America, and some in Israel. And he was going to make a reunion for us, the surviving children, to meet with Luba in Amsterdam. [PAUSES] He asked me, would I come?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine my reply to him. Although I had already arranged with my cousin John in England to go to the 50-year commemorative service at Belsen, I'd arranged that before I knew about this whole thing with Luba. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to England in April, beginning of April, and that was '95, last year. I went to London. My wife and my mother stayed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed a few days in London, and I flew to Amsterdam. [PAUSES] Heavy-heartedly, I flew to Amsterdam. [PAUSES] I was booked into the hotel where everybody was staying, and this Jack-- he calls himself Jack. His name is Isaac-- had done it all for everybody. [PAUSES] I arrived. I got a taxi from the airport. I went to Amsterdam. Of course, I know better than Palm Springs. [LAUGHS] And I arrived at the hotel, called the Garden Hotel, in Amsterdam. I went to the reception. I said I'm with the Luba party. And the girl said, they're having lunch over there. [PAUSES] I don't know how I walked into that restaurant in the hotel. [PAUSES] The first person I put my eyes upon was Luba, who I hadn't seen, except for the video, for 50 years. [PAUSES] It was such an emotional time for me, an emotional moment in my life. It was as emotional, I think, to the day of a liberation of the camp. As I hugged her and she hugged me, almost the next person whose face I saw was Hetty Werkendum, the girl who I knew from the camp. The girl with whom I had a crush. The girl, indeed, who took me to show where my late sister is buried. And other people, their faces came to me like I had indeed seen them a week ago. The same faces, just older. But really, the same faces. It was a terribly moving moment for me to meet them all. [PAUSES] The whole stay in Amsterdam of us children from different parts of the world, and Luba and her daughter and her husband, was all organized by a few of these children survivors. And what was organized also, that the queen of Holland, Queen Beatrix, presented through the lord mayor of Amsterdam, in the council chambers of Amsterdam, the highest honor that can be, be bestowed, a civilian honor, the highest civilian honor that can be bestowed on a non-Dutch national was bestowed in front of all of us, with all of us. And other survivors had brought their wives and grandchildren even to this meeting. The medal was bestowed upon Luba by the assistant lord mayor of Amsterdam, because Queen Beatrix herself, as a matter of fact, happened to be at that time in Israel. [PAUSES] You know, it was all over the papers, the Dutch television, et cetera. We had, one afternoon, only survivors with Luba in a room in a hotel. No husbands or wives, no children, [? nowhere. ?] Just us children survivors of the group she cared for and helped us survive. I've no doubt in my mind about that. Just with her. And we each-- [PAUSES] we each were asked to stand up and say something about ourselves, about our lives since then. And to speak with Luba in front of our other children, which was terribly moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure. [PAUSES] I told her that I would never have believed that I would see her again. I would never have believed that we would see each other again. [PAUSES] I didn't know, even now, after 50 years, how-- because words would not suffice-- how I could say to her on behalf of my mother, 90 years old, on behalf of myself, a thank you for what she did, whatever she did, to help my sister and to help me and the rest of us children who were present that afternoon. It was terribly hard, terribly difficult. [PAUSES] And [PAUSES] I look upon that now as sort of a completion of a sort of a circle. I don't know whether it has benefit to me or not. It is something that I'm more than delighted I did, to see her again and the other children. But I think, in many ways, it has reopened the wound. And indeed, of course, after my visit-- well, I must tell you also that while we had the visit in Amsterdam, the second night's seder fell in 1995 [PAUSES] exactly on the 15th of April. So in a synagogue in Amsterdam, we had the second night's seder-- Luba, us survivors, and our families that had come to that, although I came alone. We had celebrate the second night's seder exactly, to the day, 50 years since our liberation from the camp. So that was very, very moving and significant to all of us that were there. [PAUSES] After that, I left. I came back to England, because Terry, my wife, and my mother had arrived in England. And I was due to go with Terry, my cousin John, and his wife to Belsen, to the commemorative service in Belsen. And while I was in Belsen, I retraced the steps of 50 years ago. I went to the same platform where the train arrived from Westerbork. That platform, that siding, is still there today, like it was 50 years ago. The baker's shop in Bergen that supplied the bread to the camp, that baker shop, still there, in the hands of the same family. [PAUSES] The hospital, the camp buildings, are still there, still controlled by the British, as they were in 1945. And Hetty Werkendum, who also came to Belsen after Amsterdam, took me to the small clearing in the army camp in Bergen where my late sister is buried. Not in a mass grave, but in a clearing within the compound of the camp, of the army camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're back, 50 years later. [PAUSES] You come home, [PAUSES] and here we are, telling your story. Is there any thought or any words of wisdom that you would like to leave [PAUSES] to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to say [PAUSES] in the first instance, in the first instance, [PAUSES] as I was affected by what I've told by me being Jewish, I want to say that what happened to the Jewish people in the Second World War is basically the same as has happened throughout our history, whether it was the Egyptians or the Assyrians. [PAUSES] And what I want to say to young people today, know your past history, so that you have a chance for it not to be repeated again. Because I say to you that there are as many anti-Semites in the world today as there were before, and maybe even more. And what happened in Germany to the Jewish people emanated from Germany to the Jewish people, and educated people, the Germans. It can happen again anywhere, anywhere at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, I want to thank you for your story. And then I would like to bring your wife and your mother in and speak to them also.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Raynor, would you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My wife, Terry Jane, and my mother, Cissie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cissie, we partially heard your story here. And of course, I heard your full story before. [PAUSES] What do you think about Maurice doing testimony now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bit worried about it, because I know how it affects him. It affected me that night. I had a big nightmare, which I still get from time to time. But that's, that's how it is. So I was worried about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How important is it to you that he does that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a way. I think it's important that people should know what happened in those years. And I hope, please God, that it never happens again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terry, [PAUSES] you must tell us, how does it feel to live in a family with such a past? How does it affect your life? Or does it affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it certainly does affect my life, especially in regards to [PAUSES] Maurice's peace of mind, and the day-to-day living and the stresses of it all. [PAUSES] I hate to see him be in such pain [PAUSES] daily, from a song to a thought in anything [PAUSES] in the day. It brings it all forward for him. And I hate seeing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, in some way. [PAUSES] But it's amazing how well he does go on and get on with his life. But the pain is there, and the sleepless nights are there, and it's ongoing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, your final words to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES] I feel very often guilty, that by marrying Terry, she has the burden of my past. Also has the burden of my mother's life, as we live together for the last 14 years. I feel guilty about that, because of my great love for my wife, and my great love for my mother. I, in many ways, of course, feel in the middle, and try and do right for my mother, and certainly try and do right for my wife, as both are most important to me, because both are my life. My life would be nothing without either of them. So I hope to have my mother a long time. And I hope, of course, to have a long time myself with my wife. But it is very difficult, because I feel somehow [PAUSES] that I have put my and my mother's past and our everyday living it, re-living it in so many different ways, that I've put that upon my wife, which is a big, heavy burden for her. And I will always, to my dying day-- words will not describe how I feel that my wife is my wife and remains my wife under these very, very-- at times, very difficult circumstances. But she has a [YIDDISH], and that counts. The [YIDDISH], that's what it's all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I personally-- and, of course, in the name of the Shoah Foundation-- thank you, all three of you, for letting us come into your home, disrupt it, of course, and for your story, which is invaluable. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Rene.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, taken possibly in England on a visit to my grandmother when I was, I would say, about three years old, in the year 1935. This is a school photograph taken in Amsterdam, Holland, possibly in the beginning of World War II, of myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, and my late sister, Helen Rabbie. This is a photograph taken in the zoological gardens in Amsterdam, approximately, I would say, in 1938, '39. It is myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor. Next to me is my late sister, Helen Rabbie. Between the two children is my late father, Isaac Rabbie. To my right, as you're looking at the picture, is my mother, Cissie Rabbe Rabbie. And beneath her is her late sister, Anna Fransman Rabbe. This photograph was taken in September 1937 at the Schiphol Airport, which is the main airport of Amsterdam. And on this photograph you will see a little girl with a white coat. That is my late sister, Helen Rabbie. To her right is myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor. The gentleman holding the head of my sister is my late uncle, Sam Rabbe, my mother's eldest brother. Next to him, on his right, is my mother, Cissie Rabbe Rabbie. And two persons over to the right, in the dark suit with the tie and white shirt underneath the second propeller, is my late father, Isaac Rabbie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Will that do you? My sister in the white coat or, or, or dress, Helen Rabbie. Holding her head is my mother's late eldest brother, Sam Rabbe. Next to my sister, in the light-colored coat, myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor. Behind me, my mother to my left-- as you're looking at the photograph, to my right is my mother, Cissie. Behind me, directly behind me, is my aunt, [PAUSES] Mari Rabbe, the wife of my late uncle. And next to her, with the dark suit and the white shirt and tie, my late father, Isaac Rabbie. Taken at Schiphol Airport, Amsterdam, in 1936.
